item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted in the hopes that one day it will be modified.

I know this is an entry for a contest that was all about going big, but in the end it is a very good article if it stays on a manageable level.

A lot of detail could have been worked in about the -1 instances, with longer experiments on each one to better highlight the concept of "grief through the wrong lenses". Their animosity is flat, but I think it is an effect of this part simply not receiving enough attention.

On the other hand, this neglect happened for the author to give more focus on the last piece and its world-ending effects, but on the overall picture of the article this world-ending effect was shoehorned in. It is unnecessary, strange and deleterious to the overall piece.

TL;DR: take out the XK, the last painting and the Keter-class, fine-tune what is left, it is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Elijah appeared (as a -1 instance). Since the frame was made for him, this instance was stable.

She had her husband back and lived with him for decades.

This particular instance existed all the way from the 30s and beyond her passing, so this "forever young Elijah" made of grief was the haunt of the mansion.

It ceased existing when the painting was stolen, prompting the -2 event that destroyed the mansion and resulted in the frame's apprehension.

No anomalous effect made her forget anything, just the sheer shock, followed by happiness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked that letter, especially the final portion. Beautifully written. This SCP has a lovely theme that is conveyed so creatively through living paintings. Just marvelous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the majority of this, but i feel like the nothingness painting is a bit needlessly negligent. They had perfectly safe containment already, and it seems stupid to me that - with the knowledge of the thing doing terrible things when it cant produce anything- they would put something like the nothing image into it.

I notice the author seems to have deleted their account, but i still gotta -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Untitled (Black on Grey)

We know it isn't round, or square, or green or silver.

Hold up. What?

The current SCP-3013 instance appears to possess some form of anti-memetic effect; it is believed that a total of ██ Foundation personnel were sent into the current SCP-3013-1 instance to retrieve SCP-3013, before an investigation by [REDACTED] determined that Foundation personnel were systematically forgetting the existence of any personnel being sent into the containment facility.

I have to ask: Is the reference intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the authorship is no longer clear from this: this skip was written by me, Tufto, on my first account prior to my deleting it. You can find more of my stuff here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So what caused the woman to stop writing the letter?

My guess is that she got a picture of Elijah, put it in the frame, and the effect kick in, and said effect caused her to forget about the letter.

Anyone can enlighten me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading your description of what's wrong with this showed me why I thought this was off. There appeared to be no real reason that everything just ended violently with some poor excuse. "There are two of me" "I am a failure." etc when it was actually just 'not a picture of Elijah'
A picture of Elijah that isn't sad, and/or a reason why they didn't just put a picture of Elijah would probably have been a good way to alleviate a little of that…though honestly, psul is correct that grief!=violence and everything felt kind of samey.
I was thinking about downvoting, and I usually read the comments to see if I missed something, unfortunately I do think I will be downvoting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand the intended mechanism, but I'd suggest that grief, or even emotional disturbance, doesn't inevitably lead to violence. I think that a more realistic exploration of reactions to grief, even misplaced grief, would make for a much better story.

Although you'll need to work a little on smoothing down the rough edges of the explanation for the anomaly. Presumably the frame didn't create an instance of Elijah that was sad, so why does it have that effect on other pictures? The whispered explanation from the mysterious stranger seems too forced and convenient, blatantly tying together the frame's original intended effect with its current anomaly in a way that is clearly for the service of plot mechanics, rather than having a sensible in-universe explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd take objection to the idea that it's just "and then they attack". The whole point is that the frame can only portray a distorted image, because it's a channel for the creator's grief over Elijah's death, but without a picture of Elijah it doesn't know what to do. So it just creates versions of the painting through the prism of a grief it can't understand, because it doesn't know what it's grieving for; they end up being emotionally disturbed or somehow distorted from the paintings' meaning. If that makes sense.

Though, looking at it again, they are all a bit more violent than I'd initially intended… might change that at some point.

Otherwise, fair enough. Sorry you didn't like it :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of all the narrative possibilities of a picture frame that makes pictures come to life, "and then they attack" is the least interesting. There are some other issues: the testing log goes on too long, the note about replacing the irresponsible doctor is bizarre when the next supervisor takes an even bigger risk, and there are odd phrasing choices (eg "my niece Harry" - ?). But the decision to makes this a picture frame that kills you is the one that is fatal to any possibility of interest, and the window dressing of backstory isn't enough to redeem it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“You do not belong here” and “you are not Arcadian”

This got me. :D Of course, you found a way to end the world with a Mark Rothko painting, I've gotta love it!

One point, there's no need in experiment 6 to point out twice that the guy was panicking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea was that the new doctor, with his fame stemming from successful terminations, was much less experimentally-minded than Dr. F, and would seek to find the most direct way to permanently contain it- hence a picture about death with a very pure visual style, that would lack complications and simply subdue 3013 easily. I envisioned the new doctor as being brusque, arrogant and a little thoughtless, essentially.

I agree, though, that isn't very clear in the text- it was so set in my head that I neglected to elaborate on it in the text. I'll try to think of a way to demonstrate that better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The one thing here that bothered me is that the foundation transferred a new researcher to the picture frame that brings paintings to life because they thought he would be more responsible…

…and the first thing he does is put an abstract painting about death in it. I know the logic behind this was explained in the subject of that experiment, but I still didn't see why anyone thought it would be a good idea.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Thanks- shall edit now.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Subject: A copy of the painting “Pine Trees” by the 16th century Japanese artist Hasegawa Tohaku. After the results of Experiment SCP-3013-4, a desire to examine further landscape paintings is ongoing.

Please correct this to read:

Subject: A copy of the painting “Pine Trees” by the 16th century Japanese artist Hasegawa Tohaku. After the results of Experiment SCP-3013-5, a desire to examine further landscape paintings is ongoing.

Also, this SCP gave me the spooks +1

item.title: Nice!
item.content:encoded: Pretty cool concept! Liked the number of twists and turns it had too. Super interesting, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the SCPs that pulls the thin, fragile skin off the surface of 'Really'™ and shows the squirming horror underneath.
+1

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
…You know, I kept looking at the experiment logs and thinking that something was wrong with them, but for some reason I never realised that was the problem.

Shall edit immediately. Thanks for pointing that out.

Interesting article.

If I were you, I would move all of the experiments into a single collapsible Experiment Log. Having 8 collapsibles all in a row is something of a discouragement; one assumes there's a lot of text to read from them and doesn't feel motivated.

item.title: A lot of effort put into this
item.content:encoded: Really liked this and thought that the amount of work that you put in was well worth a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope this works. At least it's not a horrible mess like the one I tried yesterday. I'm still not sure I've tied it all together thematically.

Thanks to those in chat who gave me feedback.

Picture is from here, and is under a creative commons license.
